Light had been fishing for something. What he was looking for, B couldn’t say. It made it difficult to deny him what he wanted, but he certainly hoped he’d achieved it, anyway.  

  

When he’d gone in circles enough with that line of thinking, he turned instead to the query he’d been playing with since he first realized what Kira was by watching the news. How did Kira kill?  

  

He’d been trying to discover his own answer to that question since he first decided to confront Light. It was very difficult to battle someone whose tactics you were unfamiliar with. B had made an informal bargain for his life early on by offering Light something he knew he would be rabid for, information on L. Still, he’d only decided to confront him directly after finding that interesting bit of paper.  

  

B continued to return to that paper, no matter how much time had passed. He’d known from the minute he realized it had been disposed of in a hurry that it was significant. After taping it back together piece by agonizing piece, he’d been excited to see it had a small list of Kira’s victim’s names, some with dates beside them, in what he would bet was Light’s handwriting.  

  

At first, he’d thought it was only important because it was evidence, but no matter how he thought about it, he couldn’t fathom why Light would write those names down and risk leaving such evidence in the first place. He’d gone through several theories, including Light keeping track of who he killed and when, to Light straight-up making a hit list ahead of time that he was working off.  

  

But it simply didn’t make sense.  

  

He would expect that kind of thing from someone dumb, who didn’t think about the consequences if it was found or who was arrogant enough to believe no one would ever find it. Light wasn’t either of those things. B wouldn’t be surprised if Light already had a plausible explanation prepared for if that did happen, but he similarly didn’t strike B as the type to take unnecessary risks like that.  

  

That stupid piece of paper became more mysterious the longer he pondered it. Maybe it was just him making a mountain out of a molehill, but he became more and more convinced that the paper was significant in some way that he was clearly missing. But what was it? Did Light really destroy the paper in the explosion because the names were written in his handwriting or was there something else he was worried about? Did it have something to do with how Kira killed? And if it did, what role did it play?  

  

Then, a thought suddenly occurred to B and he would've cracked himself in the face for not thinking of it sooner if his hands were free.  If Light was in the habit of writing down the names of his victims, given his death count, why should there only be one paper?  

  

Perhaps there wasn't.  

  

Then the door rattled and B blinked as someone pushed the door to his cell open again, only it wasn’t Light this time. It was Wammy.  

  

And B grinned.  

  

 ------------- 

  

Watari felt there was something suspicious about L’s request. He had no proof, of course, but L’s countenance had seemed off. Regardless, he’d done as he was asked. He would look into it when he got back. Almost as soon as he returned, he went to the surveillance room he’d set up in and checked the camera feed at the time he’d been gone. Of course, there was something wrong with the cameras during that time. How convenient. On a hunch, he went to B’s cell. It took him only a moment to realize something was wrong.  

  

B’s blindfold was off.  

  

Watari was certain he’d been blindfolded when he left. Someone else had been here. “Did someone visit you?” Watari asked B coolly.  

  

B gave him a wide grin. “If someone did, who did you think it would be?”  

  

Watari knew who it had been. He knew with utter certainty. Light Yagami was proving to be a pain in his hide, and he could feel his frustration boiling over in that moment even as he kept an iron grip on his calm. L and his successors were compromised and he was helping Light scuttle around here, undermining him.  

  

He needed to have Light dealt with tonight before anything else had the chance to happen. Before he could ruin his work any further. 

 

That was the only option left to him now.  

Chapter End Notes

If it feels like the story is approaching its climax, that’s because it is.

Also, I promise that all hell is about to break loose. (This promise coming from the same author who blew up a building for no particular reason.)

Chapter 55: All Hell Breaks Loose

Chapter Summary

What it says on the tin.

Chapter Notes

I’m here! I’m alive!! These last three months have been…a lot. But I’m here and I have the promised chapter! Here ya go!

The air in the room didn't change temperature, but for Light, it felt as though someone had opened a freezer door.

“Light, you’re back. Did you get what Ryuzaki needed?” Touta asked, but Light couldn’t focus on what he said because he was looking at Misa and Misa was looking at him with wide eyes like she’d just seen the most amazing thing in her life. He knew that look. 

 

“That’s Light?” Misa asked, her eyes blown wide and glittering. Light felt a shiver down his spine. 

 

The situation was awkward with Light just standing there in the doorway, but he couldn’t bring himself to move. Naturally, several people noticed his distress. L’s gaze sharpened on Light and Misa, sensing the strange energy. Soichiro and Naomi straightened in their seats. Touta, looking confused but concerned, got up from his seat to approach Light. He grabbed Light’s wrist and pulled him to sit on the couch beside him, avoiding immediate proximity to Misa, who was - as usual - positioned next to L. 

 

Next to his friend, Light almost felt himself relax and then Misa’s excited voice broke through the room and Light’s tension ratcheted back up again. 

 

It wasn't the heat. It was the noise. Not auditory noise—though Misa Amane was certainly capable of that—but the sheer, vibrating chaotic energy of her existence. Light sat perfectly still on the sofa, his hands resting on his knees, forcing his breathing to remain even. To the rest of the Task Force, she was a pop idol, a witness, a bubbly young woman caught up in a tragedy.

 

To Light, she was a memory of a noose. She was a reminder of a life where he had been a god of the new world, and she had been his most devoted, destructive acolyte.

 

"I just can't believe I finally get to see you properly!" Misa chirped. She leaned forward, invading his personal space with a scent of vanilla that made Light’s stomach turn, the memories nearly overwhelming. "Wow! You’re so handsome! Why would you wear a mask all this time?? I didn’t know you looked like this under the mask!"

 

Light flinched. It was microscopic, a mere twitch of the muscle in his jaw, but he felt his Shinigami nature itch at the back of his mind. He wanted to shorten her lifespan, to say “screw it” to not further retaliating against someone he’d wronged in the past. Stop it, he commanded himself. You aren’t that impulsive, violent child anymore.

 

But the trauma of his previous life—the life where Misa’s devotion had been a suffocating weight that strangled him—was screaming. He felt cornered. He remembered her eyes, the deal, the way she would kill without hesitation if he so much as frowned but would never stop pressing herself against his body and invading his personal space when he practically begged for it.

 

"Misa," Light said, his voice sounding hollow to his own ears. "Please. Personal space."

 

"Oh, don't be shy!" She reached out, her manicured hand aiming for his arm.

 

Before her fingers could graze the fabric of his shirt, a pale, long-fingered hand intercepted her wrist. It wasn't aggressive, not exactly, but the grip was absolute. L had moved from his desk chair to perch on the arm of the couch next to Light astonishingly without Light noticing, or maybe that was just a result of the state he was in now. 

 

"Ms. Amane," L’s voice was a monotone drone, but Light knew that tone. He could hear the underlying tone. He was irritated and this was a warning. "Light is currently recovering from a significant ordeal. His tolerance for physical touch is... limited."

 

L didn't let go of her wrist. He sat perched on the arm of the sofa next to Light, looming over him like a gargoyle protecting its cathedral. Light looked up, catching the way L’s dark eyes bore into Misa. There was something dark swirling in those pupils, a distinct protectiveness that suddenly made the room feel a few degrees warmer and much safer. 

 

Misa managed to pick up on some of the annoyance from L as he finally released her wrist, although Light felt sure she didn’t get just how much irritation was radiating from L. "Oh, I'm sorry, Kira! I was just trying to be friendly! Maybe he needs a woman's touch to feel better! People always tell me how cheerful I am!"

 

"I assure you," L said, shifting his weight so that he was physically blocking a portion of Light from Misa’s view, "Light’s needs are being adequately met. We do not require... external assistance."

 

The double meaning hung in the air, sharp as a guillotine blade; a blade that only Misa couldn’t see. Matsuda, sitting awkwardly next to this circus of a love triangle, choked on air.

 

Light finally found his voice. He shifted, leaning almost imperceptibly into L’s side. The contact—the sharp angle of L’s hip bone against his shoulder—grounded him. "Ryuzaki is right, Misa. I’m not... I’m not up to talking with people I don’t know well right now."

 

People he doesn’t know well. As if he hadn’t spent years manipulating her and fending her off, in equal measure. As if he didn’t know exactly how she kissed, how she cried, how she died. Guilt washed over him, cold and slimy. He had used her. In another life, he had destroyed her after having thrown her into danger for his own needs numerous times. And now, she was here, looking at him with those big, innocent eyes, that saw a boy her age that looked like a fairytale prince and already forming an attachment that only made his stomach turn and gave him a different slimy feeling. 

 

“Ryuzaki is just very concerned about Light.” Matsuda said, glancing between Light and L like he wasn’t sure if he should tell Misa the whole story between them. 

 

"I think he's sweet!" Misa beamed, oblivious to the undercurrents of treason and suspicion. “I’ll stay overnight to help him cheer Light up then! When I went through your little TSA check at the front entrance today, Watari suggested I stay the night to get to know everyone better!” 

 

L’s eyes narrowed. "Watari is very generous," He murmured, bringing his thumb to his lip. "Perhaps too generous."

 

Light, bolstered by L’s defense of him, had the bandwidth to think about how incredibly suspicious that was. Watari usually faded into the background unless he was specifically ordered to do something. It wasn’t his place to suggest something like that. More than that, Watari knew L’s psychological profile better than anyone; he knew L worked best in isolation, and he knew Light was the center of L’s current emotional stability. Suggesting Misa stay the night wasn't an act of kindness. It was a disruption tactic. It was throwing a grenade into a calm pond to see what floated to the surface. Light already knew that Watari hated how emotionally invested L had become in Light, so he wasn’t surprised that this was the man’s latest trick. Most worrying was the potential that Watari was weaponizing Light’s clear discomfort with Misa, exposing him to her more to destabilize him and therefore L. 

 

"Did he?" Light asked softly. He’s trying to separate us mentally before he separates us physically.

 

"He did!" Misa spun in a circle, her skirt flaring. "And I'm going to do my best! Now, Light, tell me everything. Kira barely talks about you. What do you like? Do you like sweets? I’ve seen you snacking on apples. I bet you're the healthy type."

 

She was a barrage. A sensory assault. Light felt his heart rate spiking, the fight-or-flight response kicking in. He couldn't breathe. He needed to get away from her, but he couldn't leave the room without looking suspicious.

 

Suddenly, L uncoiled from his perch. He stood up, towering over Misa, and then, with deliberate slowness, turned his back on her. He faced Light, extending a hand.

 

"Light, I need you to come with me for a moment." L said.

 

"Where?" Light asked, though he was already reaching out.

 

"Bedroom," L stated flatly. He turned his head slightly, addressing the room at large. "Light requires rest. Ms. Amane, Matsuda will entertain you. Do not disturb us."

 

"What? He’s leaving? You’re going with him? But why?" Misa protested, taking a step forward.

 

L turned back, his eyes wide and unblinking, the black depths of them devoid of any warmth for her. "He is my partner. He is my responsibility. And frankly Ms. Amane, you are being loud."

 

He didn't wait for a response. He pulled Light up from the sofa and dragged him toward the hallway, away from the vanilla scent, away from the noise, away from the reminder of the Kira he used to be. Rather than vanilla, now he could only smell strawberries.

 

As the door to the investigation room clicked shut, cutting off Misa’s confused question of “he means partner in the investigation, right?”, and the silence rushed back in. But it wasn't a peaceful silence. It was the silence of a pressure cooker. L pulled Light along silently to their private quarters, Light not speaking until they got back to their room and the door closed. 

 

Light leaned back against the closed door, exhaling a breath he felt he’d been holding for ten minutes. "She doesn't stop."

 

"No," L said. He was standing in the middle of the room, not looking at Light. He was staring at the floor, his toes curling into the carpet. "She is... persistent. I was aware of this from her obsession with me as ‘Kira’, however, her sudden and immediate attention towards you is…surprising." Annoying. 

 

"She has a crush," Light said, rubbing his temples. "I can see it in her eyes. It’s... it’s going to be a problem, L."

 

L turned then. He moved quickly, getting into Light’s personal space, though a fraction less intrusively than Misa had. But this time, Light didn't flinch. He didn't recoil. “Is that why you insisted on wearing the mask?” L asked and Light didn’t have it in him to lie to his boyfriend. 

 

Light nodded. “I had a strong feeling that Misa was the kind of shallow person who would fall for someone for their looks. I know I’m handsome and a lot of girls have formed crushes on me at school despite barely talking to me. We already knew she was a borderline dangerously obsessive person, so I wanted to hide my face to avoid that.”

 

There was a brief silence as L considered that viewpoint. "Then we will have to make it clear that we are in a relationship. Ms. Amane seems the type that would balk at being an affair partner. However, if she persists, I will block her access to this building and I’ll expedite a restraining order."

 

Light looked into L’s eyes and felt a relief so strong that it nearly sent him crumbling down on his knees. Those words rivaled any romantic overture Light himself had made, he was sure of it. He really needed to hear that and he found himself crushingly grateful for L and all his L-ness. If anyone outside the team saw the two together now, they would probably be stunned. Light looked like a morally upright and perfect prodigy and he knew how L looked to others, how he had looked to Light when they first met a lifetime ago. They would be in disbelief that Light would want the reclusive, insomniac detective and the darkness that followed him instead of the adoring, pretty girl who is an actress and model.

 

"L Lawliet," Light said quietly, using the name that was theirs alone. "You are the most amazing man in the world. I adore you."

 

L rested his forehead against Light’s and closed his eyes, looking pained. "She said that Watari…"

 

"I know.” Light said, sympathetically. He understood. L was trying so hard to believe in the man who was a father figure to him, the person that had been his right-hand man in everything he had done this far, and it was an uphill battle. Light shoved down the part of himself that wanted to rush L into accepting that Watari was a selfish snake that was sabotaging him and instead rested his hand on L’s back, pulling him closer. Because that was what L really needed right now. “I know.”

 

~ 🅚 ~

 

Several floors below the warm, suffocating tension of the living quarters, the air was recycled and scrubbed until it smelled of absolutely nothing.

 

Watari walked down the corridor of the containment cell. The click of his shoes on the linoleum was rhythmic, precise, and utterly devoid of hesitation. In his hands, he carried a tablet, the screen displaying the biometric data of the task force members above. He was reviewing the footage of L taking Light from the investigation room to their living quarters, where his cameras had been removed. 

 

They were together. Again.

 

It was made worse by the fact that he couldn’t see what they were doing in there, but he could imagine. 

 

Watari stopped in front of a heavy steel door, his expression unmoving. For twenty-five years, he had dedicated his existence to L. He had taken L in, bought him everything he needed, aided him in his every endeavor, built up the orphanage, filtered the candidates, and hardened the successors. He had been the shield and sword for the greatest detective the world had ever known. He had done it all to nurture L’s genius, to keep it pure, uncorrupted by the messy, illogical failings of human emotion.

 

And in a matter of months, Light Yagami had undone it all. He would’ve been impressed by the efficiency of how quickly this teenage boy managed to undo his years of work if it wasn’t such an insult to everything he’d spent his life working for. 

 

It wasn't partnership; it was dependency. Dependency on the unreliability of unstable affection that meant nothing to the world in the end. Ryuzaki had lost his objectivity. He was no longer looking for the truth; he wasn’t even looking for a way to exonerate the boy he was infatuated with. He was looking for a way to pin the blame for the boy’s crimes somewhere else so he could…what? Run off into the sunset together and leave behind the reputation they’d both worked for? Watari’s perfect project? L’s life work?

 

If the limb is gangrenous, Watari thought, sliding the tablet into his coat pocket, one does not bargain with the infection. One amputates.

 

He keyed the security code into the panel on the wall. It was a complex sequence, one that only he and Ryuzaki knew. The heavy tumblers inside the door shifted with a dull thud, a sound that vibrated in the floorboards.

 

Watari didn't open the door immediately. He spoke to the intercom, his voice flat. "I know you are awake."

 

"Sleep is for the boring, Quillsh," a voice crackled back through the speaker. It was a voice that sounded like wet silk dragging over gravel. "And I have been so... remarkably bored. Bored enough to start getting ideas."

 

Watari pushed the door open.

 

The containment cell was stark white. Beyond Birthday was just as Watari had left him approximately half an hour ago. His blindfold remained off on the table next to where B was tied. B was wired, vibrating with a manic energy. His black hair was a bird's nest of spikes, and his eyes glittered in the harsh fluorescent light and he was smiling.

 

B tilted his head, a slow, predatory movement. “Coming for another visit so soon, Quillsh? Ready to make good on your delightful threats?"

 

Without a word, Watari stepped inside, letting the door hiss shut behind him, though he didn't lock it. Not this time. 

 

Watari wordlessly started unlocking B’s restraints and for the first time in a while, B went perfectly still, though the predatory look in his eyes and his smile remained. His eyes were like a hawk’s as he watched the belts release, one by one. As he was fully freed and Watari took a professional step back, B’s grin widened, stretching the skin of his face unnervingly. “My, my. What would L say if he knew his faithful hound was going off-leash?"

 

"He will see reason eventually. He will see that everything I do is for his best interests." Watari said. He stood perfectly still, hands folded behind his back. "He is currently unable to see the threat standing directly in front of him."

 

B stepped directly in front of Watari, whispering the name of their universal target. "Light Yagami."

 

"Yes."

 

"The Boyfriend." B giggled, a sharp, staccato sound. "It eats you up inside, doesn't it? Knowing that your precious masterpiece has been stained by a common serial killer."

 

"He is not common in his threat level." Watari corrected, turning to face B. "He is a threat to global security. And more importantly, he is a threat to everything he has worked for. He isn’t thinking clearly now, but once Light’s influence is removed, he will recover his senses over time. He would grieve but he would eventually see that this is for the best."

 

B hummed, his eyes narrowing and his smile drooping a little for the moment as he glared at Watari. “Right. For the best. Grief is just something that will…go away.” Then his smile was back and he shrugged, the movement stiff with how restrained his movements have been over the last several days. “But what do I care about your reasons? You know I’m after my own gratification here and I’m the tool you’re using to get the dirty work done that you find distasteful. Don’t want L to connect this to you, right? Classic Quillsh. Always keeping your hands clean while the children play in the mud. Just let me out to do my business and cause a disturbance so I can get a move on. We’ll both get what we want.”

 

"Light and Ryuzaki are currently distracted in their room. We’ll need them separated for this and for the other investigators to be distracted. I will cause the necessary distractions and work to separate them, so try not to be a further disappointment.” Watari said, holding up an ID card that would get B through any locked doors. 

 

B snatched the card from Watari’s hand with blinding speed. He held it up to the light, admiring the chip. "And as for what happens after Light bites it?"

 

“Do you really care what happens after you have your revenge?” Watari asked, tonelessly. 

 

"No, I suppose I don’t." B mused. “Make the systems fail. Separate them. Pull Ryuzaki to one side of the building and Light to the other, and I’ll handle the rest. Can you do that?" B grinned, and for a moment, he looked truly demonic.

 

"Then go," Watari stepped aside. "The cameras in the stairwell are looped and soon the ones on the upper levels will stop recording entirely. You are invisible."

 

B walked to the door, pausing at the threshold. He looked back at Watari, his expression uncharacteristically serious for a split second. "You know he’ll hate you for this. When he finds out."

 

"He won’t find out." Watari said simply. "Light’s death will be the result of a security breach where a serial killer with a grudge got loose in the building and hacked the system. Stop wasting time and go."

 

B laughed and slipped out into the corridor. Watari stood alone in the silence of the cell for a long moment. He tapped his earpiece.

 

"Central," Watari said, his voice returning to the calm, grandfatherly tone the Task Force knew. "I am heading up to the server room to run a routine diagnostic on the firewall. I’ve noticed some... irregularities."

 

"Copy that, Watari," Ide’s voice came through, oblivious and trusting.

 

Watari walked out of the cell, leaving the door slightly ajar. The game had changed. It was no longer a detective solving a case. It was a rescue mission. And Watari would save L from his own humanity, even if he had to burn the rest of the world—and Light Yagami—to ash to do it.

 

~ 🅚 ~

 

In the server room, the hum of the cooling fans was a steady, meditative drone. It was the heartbeat of the investigation, the physical manifestation of L’s reach. Watari stood before the main terminal, his fingers hovering over the command line.

 

He didn't need to hack the system. He was the system.

 

He typed in a command: OVERRIDE_AUTH_PROTOCOL_00. 

 

Then, INITIATE_LOCKDOWN_TEST_SEQUENCE_RANDOM.

 

He pressed Enter.

 

The screens in front of him flickered. The steady green status bars turned a violent, blinking amber. Somewhere deep in the bowels of the building, a heavy hydraulic pump groaned.

 

In the bedroom, the air was still thick with the tension of the earlier confrontation. Light and L were on the bed, trying to rest after the latest ordeal even though Light was still agitated by Misa’s presence.

 

“Light, about your reaction to Misa.” L started. “It seems more than a little bit pointed. I have some theories about it.” 

 

"And I’d love to hear your theories, dear, but now’s not a good—" Light started.

 

CLACK.

 

The sound was loud, like a gunshot, coming from the electronic lock on their bedroom door opening.

 

Before either of them could react, the overhead lights died. Total darkness swallowed the room for a heartbeat, followed immediately by the crimson pulse of the emergency strobes. The room was bathed in a rhythmic, blood-red wash of light. Flash. Dark. Flash. Dark. An alarm began to blare—not a fire alarm, but the high-pitched, oscillating wail of a security breach.

 

L was moving before the first strobe finished its cycle. He was at the door, his hand on the handle. "The magnetic seals are cycling," he said, his voice cutting through the noise.

 

"An attack?" Light asked, sliding off the bed.

 

"Unlikely," L said, his eyes reflecting the red strobe lights. "There are other sounds underneath the siren. Listen."

 

Click-thud. Click-thud. The door lock was engaging and disengaging rapidly, like a heart in fibrillation.

 

"System failure?" Light suggested, moving to L’s side. "Or Watari?"

 

"Watari would not compromise the perimeter without verbal confirmation unless he was incapacitated," L stated, though there was a flicker of hesitation in his voice. "We need to get to the control center. If the containment cells fail..."

 

"B," Light finished the thought. A cold spike of adrenaline hit him. If the power cut, the magnetic locks on the cells below might disengage.

 

"And Misa," L added, gritting his teeth. "She is with Matsuda in the common area. If the blast shutters come down, they could be trapped. We have to go."

 

The door clicked open on the cycle. L kicked it wide, and the two of them sprinted into the hallway. The corridor was a nightmare of flashing red lights and the deafening wail of the siren.

 

"Watari!" L shouted into his comms, but all that came back was static.

 

"Jamming?" Light yelled.

 

"Interference," L corrected, grabbing Light’s wrist to keep them together in the strobe-lit confusion. "Come on!"

 

They disappeared around the corner, heading toward the main elevators, their footsteps swallowed by the chaos. 

 

After they disappeared down the hall, a shadow peeled itself away from the alcove near the linen closet. Beyond Birthday stepped into the red-washed hallway. He was smiling. He loved the color red; it made everything look so... vital.

 

He had watched the two figures retreat—L, the hunchbacked genius, dragging his pretty, murderous pet by the hand. It was almost poetic. They were running to fix the machine, unaware that the machine was eating itself.

 

"Run along, little detectives," B whispered.

 

He slipped up to the door of their bedroom. The lock was still clicking wildly, latching and unlatching. B timed it. One, two, click.

 

He pushed the door open and slipped inside.

 

The noise of the alarm was muffled instantly as the heavy door swung shut. The room was still bathed in the rhythmic red flashing of the emergency strobe in the corner, giving the space the feeling of a developing photograph in a darkroom. And the picture to B looked like victory. Finally.

 

B took a deep breath and then gagged theatrically. "Disgusting," he hissed.

 

The room smelled of them. It was a suffocating cocktail of scents. L’s sharp, sugary smell—like ozone and strawberry cake. And underneath it, the crisp, clean, expensive scent of Light Yagami. Soap, starch, and something metallic.

 

It was the smell of domesticity. Like L thought he could ruin people’s lives and then get all domestic with the world’s most prolific serial killer. 

 

B walked to the bed. The sheets were rumpled. Two pillows. They slept here. They slept together. The great L, the world’s greatest mind, sleeping next to a mass murderer, sharing warmth, sharing breath.

 

"Look at you," B sneered at the empty bed, running a hand over the duvet. "Playing house. Playing human."

 

It made his skin crawl. It was a debasement of everything L was supposed to be. Everything the successors were taught to be. Their human connections had always been treated as failings on their part. And here was L, indulging in the thing they were all denied. Light Yagami came along and suddenly it was alright to have... feelings. To be soft.

 

B snatched a pillow—Light’s pillow—and threw it across the room as he struggled to suppress his rage at the injustice of it all. It hit the wall with a soft thud, completely unheard under the raging sirens.

 

"Focus," he chided himself. "Treasure hunt."

 

He moved to the desk. Papers were stacked in neat piles (Light’s doing) next to a precarious tower of sugar cubes (L’s doing). B swept the sugar cubes onto the floor with a crash, ignoring the mess. He rifled through the drawers. Case files. Wigs. Handcuffs. B raised an eyebrow at those, a lewd grin touching his lips, before tossing them aside.

 

Nothing.

 

He moved to the closet. Rows of identical white shirts for L. Rows of sharp, tailored blazers for Light.

 

"Where would you hide it, Kira?" B muttered, pushing the hangers aside. "You’re arrogant, but you’re not stupid. You wouldn't leave it in a book. You wouldn't leave it under the mattress."

 

He knelt down, checking the floorboards. Nothing loose.

 

He checked the vents. Secure.

 

He turned back to the room, his eyes scanning the flickering space. He tried to think like Light Yagami. Light was paranoid. Light expected to be watched. Light knew Watari was watching him, but he also knew L was on his side in this. So, the hiding spot had to be something someone would dismiss as mundane.

 

B’s eyes landed on the bedside table. Specifically, on a sleek, black leather watch case.

 

He’d seen Light wearing a watch earlier. An expensive one. A gift from his father, perhaps? Wait, hadn’t Light in the video been wearing that watch, too, when B successfully baited him into killing before? It would be just like Light to have a hidden compartment in something obvious, waving it in front of everyone on his wrist every day. Hidden in plain sight.

 

B walked over and flipped the lid of the watch case. It was empty, save for the velvet cushion. Of course. Light was wearing it. But if it was there, that solved B’s problem. If Light had another of those pages - assuming he was correct about the one he had not being the only one - then he could kill two birds with one stone and then he could find out what was so important to that paper from Light himself. 

 

"Watari," B said, tapping the comms unit he’d swiped from the containment guard earlier. "I’m done here.” 

 

"Proceed to the rendezvous," Watari’s voice came back, crackling through the static. "I am escalating the failure. The blast doors will seal in sixty seconds."

 

"Escalating," B chuckled. He looked around the room one last time, at the rumpled bed, the spilled sugar, the life these two had built.

 

He grabbed a red pen off the desk and proceeded to the mirror in the connecting bathroom. In jagged, violent strokes, he wrote across the glass:

 

YOUR SECURITY IS A JOKE. - B

 

He capped the pen, dropped it on the floor, and slipped out of the room just as the lock clicked shut again, leaving the sanctuary as a monument of a life they could’ve lived together and a promise of secrets to be stolen.

 

~ 🅚 ~

 

The hallway was a throat, and they were being swallowed.

 

The emergency sirens had desynchronized, creating a cacophony of wailing dissonance that vibrated in Light’s teeth. The red strobes were relentless, carving the world into jagged slices of time. Flash: L running ahead, his spine curved, phone pressed to his ear. Dark. Flash: The blast shutters on the windows slamming down, sealing them inside a steel tomb. Dark.

 

"Central is dark," L shouted over the noise, skidding around a corner. "The hardlines are cut. This isn't a malfunction, Light. This is a siege."

 

Light was right on his heels. "The panic room. Sector 4!"

 

"No," L stopped abruptly, grabbing Light’s shoulder. "If the systems are compromised, the panic room is a coffin. We need to get to the secondary server farm. I need to manually override the lockdown."

 

"That’s through three security checkpoints," Light yelled.

 

"Then we run," L said, looking at Light with rare, naked intensity. "Stay close to me. Do not let go."

 

They sprinted. They reached the heavy blast doors separating the residential wing from the technical wing. L slapped his palm against the biometric scanner.

 

BEEP. AUTHORIZED.

 

The heavy gears groaned, and the door began to slide open—slowly.

 

"Go," L commanded, shoving Light toward the widening gap.

 

Light stumbled through, catching himself on the other side. He turned back, expecting L to follow.

 

But L froze. Above the door, the status light flickered from Green to Red.

 

BEEP. SECURITY LOCKDOWN INITIATED.

 

The door reversed course. It slammed shut with terrifying speed, sealing the corridor with a sound like a thunderclap.

 

"L!" Light lunged forward, slamming his hands against the cold metal. He frantically jammed his own ID card against the scanner.

 

BZZT. ACCESS DENIED. USER ID INVALID.

 

Light stared at the red screen. Invalid? His clearance was Level 5. It was hard-coded into the mainframe.

 

"Think," Light hissed to himself. He backed away from the door, his heart hammering against his ribs. This is too pointed for an error, it reeked of calculation. This was meant to separate us.

 

Light couldn’t hear if L was calling out for him, the heavy door between them and the migraine inducing screech of the alarms made it incredibly difficult to even hear his own yelling. The red lights settled into a steady, dim crimson glow. Light felt more than heard a presence behind him and his eyes snapped behind him. 

 

Ten meters away, standing at the intersection, was Watari. He wore his pristine trench coat, his hands folded neatly behind his back.

 

"Watari," Light growled out, his eyes briefly flashing red, something that was invisible in the red tinted hallway. Light didn’t need to ask because he knew. 

 

For a moment, Watari was motionless. Then from behind his back, he produced a matte black tactical pistol with a silencer. He leveled the barrel directly at Light’s chest. Light froze. 

 

A cold wind that wasn't there rushed past Light’s ear. Ryuk materialized through the wall, his feathery shoulders hunched in agitation.

 

“Light!” Ryuk hissed, his large yellow eyes wide and unblinking. He hovered between Light and Watari, vibrating with nervous energy. “What happ--” Ryuk stopped when he saw Watari leveling a gun at Light, giving his own growl. 

 

"What are you doing?" Light asked Watari steadily, though his eyes flicked momentarily to the Shinigami. "Put the gun down."

 

"We are undergoing a purification," Watari said calmly. "You are a cancer, Light Yagami. You have infected Ryuzaki’s logic. As his handler... I cannot allow that."

 

“His handler.” Light repeated, clear disgust coloring his tone.

 

“Light, he’s serious,” Ryuk said nervously, his long fingers twitching toward the pouch at his hip where he kept his Death Note. “He’s actually thinking of shooting you.”

 

Light gives a sharp smile at the elderly man, a move that had all of the hate a god of death could muster. “L’s done everything you could’ve wanted from him and you can’t tolerate him having one good thing in his life.” 

 

Watari didn’t react to the accusation. "Ryuzaki is brilliant, Light. But he has a blind spot. You."

 

"I'm not his enemy." Light spat, glaring at the venomous old man that had ruined so many children’s lives and was now holding him at gunpoint. "I love him."

 

"I know." Watari said. "And that is why you have to go.”

 

"He'll never forgive you," Light snarled, not bothering to pretend to be entirely human anymore. He let the Chronos in him surface, cold and sharp. "If you kill me, you kill his heart. A person doesn’t forgive that."

 

"He will mourn," Watari admitted. "But he will survive. He is L. He always survives."

 

"Don't be so sure about that, Quillsh," a voice came from the ceiling.

 

Light and Ryuk both looked up at the ceiling as a vent cover above them crashed to the floor. A dark shape dropped from the shadows, landing in a crouch directly behind Light.

 

Light spun around, but he wasn't fast enough. A forearm, thin but strong as steel cable, slammed across his throat.

 

"Gotcha," Beyond Birthday whispered in his ear. He smelled of dust and old blood. As B’s arm crushed against his trachea, Light didn't just feel fear. He felt a profound, burning irony. He was a God of Death being manhandled by a failed experiment, and he had to let it happen. He had to endure this indignity because B was a puzzle he hadn't solved yet. 

 

"Get off me!" Light thrashed, trying to summon the strength of a god, but he was just a human boy with a bruised ribcage.

 

"Restrain him," Watari ordered, keeping the gun trained on them.

 

B wrenched Light’s arms behind his back. There was the metallic ratchet-click of heavy-duty law enforcement cuffs—not the standard ones, but the solid bar hinged ones used for high-risk transport. They bit into Light’s wrists, locking his arms painfully tight.

 

"Stop struggling, Kira," B crooned, leaning his weight onto Light’s back. "You look so much prettier when you submit."

 

“LIGHT!” Ryuk roared. The Shinigami whipped his Death Note out, his bone-like pen hovering over the page. “That’s it! I’m writing them! I’ll kill them both right now!”

 

Panic, colder and sharper than the knife B was surely holding, pierced Light. If Ryuk wrote their names to save Light’s life, he would be using the Note to extend a human lifespan. Maybe that rule didn’t work in Light’s case - given what he was - but maybe it did. He had no way of knowing and he wasn’t going to play Russian roulette with his oldest friend’s life. 

 

"No!" Light gasped, looking wildly at the empty air where Ryuk hovered. "Ryuk, stop! Don't do it!"

 

Ryuk paused, his pen quivering, and growled. “I won’t let you die, Light! Not again! You’ve done enough dying.”

 

"You idiot, you'll die!" Light hissed, desperate, ignoring the confused look Watari gave him. "If you save me, you turn to dust! Do not write their names!"

 

"Talking to your imaginary friends, Kira? You’ve played a lot of clever tricks, but I think the insanity defense is a bit beneath you." B laughed, a giddy, high-pitched sound right in Light’s ear. He kicked the back of Light’s knee, forcing him to the ground. Light’s chin hit the linoleum hard, tasting copper. Ryuk was hovering right above them, his expression a rictus of immortal panic and rage at these humans. He looked moments away from defying Light just to keep his friend alive, at the cost of himself.

 

Light’s mind was racing, vibrating with the adrenaline of the capture and the terror of losing Ryuk. But beneath the panic, the gears of Chronos were spinning wildly. He thought that he should’ve just said “screw the King’s warning” and killed B when he had the chance. But then, he couldn’t have killed Watari because of what that would do to L and he was even more of a threat than B. 

 

Still, the fact that he couldn’t figure out what the king had wanted him to find out from B grated at him, even in this situation. He’d risked exactly this and for what? He came away with nothing. Nothing could be worth the risk he’d taken. The only thing Light truly valued were the people in his life. The people he hadn’t appreciated in his past life. The people, the person, he loved. 

 

Wait. Values. 

 

A connection formed in Light’s mind. It was a sudden start. B valued the same thing, the personal connection, the connection to A. His mind flashed back to the interrogation room. The conversations they’d had. To B’s story. The bathtub.

 

“I stumbled upon him in the aftermath of his first attempt or maybe I should call it his first success. I’d gone to use the bathroom and this kid was in the bathtub. I panicked and pulled him out, thinking he’d drowned. I remember thinking his heart had stopped and I started doing CPR and he started coughing. It was the only time I’d seen his time change. It had all run out when I pulled him out of the tub, but he got a new number above his head when he started coughing. I thought maybe I’d saved him, but the time above his head, it wasn’t a lot and it started counting down again.”

 

Light knew the rules. The King can’t touch a human unless they are dead or dealing. He knew what the King said about only being able to turn someone into a Shinigami if they possessed features of a Shinigami in life, but that he could do that if they died and came back. Like B. Like A. 

 

This was all supposition, it depended entirely on what the King had been doing at that moment in the past, but if A had been given the Eyes... if A saw the numbers that drove him mad... he didn't get them from a deal.

 

A died in that tub, Light realized. The truth hit him like a thunderclap, drowning out B's taunts. He died. And the King was waiting.

 

The Shinigami King had taken a gamble on the First Successor. He had used that moment of death to plant a seed. Maybe it had been to pad his numbers in case B didn’t work out. Or maybe he realized that B would become close with A and it was a favor to his experiment. Maybe the King just wanted to see what would happen if two children with the eyes teamed up together. Regardless, if he had given A the Eyes before B dragged him back to the land of the living…that was why A killed himself later. He woke up seeing the dates.

 

And if A really was dead now... and if the King had marked him...

 

A is a Shinigami. He is a Shinigami right now. 

 

The thought paralyzed Light. It wasn't a theory; it was a certainty. A was out there, in the Realm. If he was like Light had been, like all the other Shinigami, then he wouldn’t remember his human life except maybe as emotional echoes. What’s more, if this was true, then it presented Light with the antidote to something Light had been refusing to think about up until now, the question of what happened when their human lives ended and Light returned to being a Shinigami…without L. 

 

And right now, that mystery Shinigami was the only solution he could think of for stopping B without sacrificing Ryuk. 

 

"B," Watari said, interrupting Light’s rapidfire thoughts. He tossed a hunting knife onto the floor, sliding it toward B. "Take him to the roof. Make sure he does not live through the night."

 

"With pleasure," B purred, snatching up the knife.

 

B grabbed Light by the back of his collar and hauled him to his feet. Light stumbled. Ryuk was still there, pen poised.

 

Light looked up at the Shinigami, his eyes burning with a sudden, fierce clarity that cut through his fear.

 

"Go," Light whispered to Ryuk, the word barely audible.

 

“What?” Ryuk balked. “Light, I can't leave you—”

 

"Go back to the Realm," Light hissed, his voice cracking as B shoved the knife tip against his ribs. "Ryuk, this is an order. Go to the King. Ask him... ask him if he has a new pet."

 

Ryuk tilted his massive head, confused. “A new pet?”

 

"Ask him about the one who died twice," Light breathed, locking eyes with the Shinigami. "I think I figured it out, Ryuk. I know what the King is hiding. Go! If you stay, we both lose."

 

Ryuk hesitated. He looked at the knife against Light’s side, then back at Light’s face. He saw the old Kira there—the calculating, gambling god.

 

“Hyuk,” Ryuk chuckled but there was no mirth in it, snapping his Death Note shut in agitation. “You’re crazy, Light. You know that? Fine. But if you die while I’m gone, I’m never forgiving you. Even if human death isn’t permanent for you, you have that life to live with the love of your lives, remember? You gotta keep living for that.”

 

With a flap of his leathery wings that stirred no air, Ryuk dissolved into mist and shot upward through the ceiling, vanishing back to the realm of death.

 

Light was alone.

 

"Come along, piggy," B whispered, yanking him backward toward the darkness of the service access. "Time for the slaughter."

 

Light didn't struggle against the handcuffs anymore. He needed to talk to B. He needed to buy time for Ryuk to return, even if it meant tempting B with answers to volatile questions. 

 

I will survive this, Light vowed as the darkness swallowed them, the red light of the corridor fading away. For L. I will survive, and I will find the Shinigami named A.

Afterword
